content:
Named distributions grew out of **games of chance** and **error theory**. **Bernoulli** and **Binomial** laws formalized coin-flip arithmetic (Jacob Bernoulli, 1713 *Ars Conjectandi*). **Poisson** (1837) described rare events as limits of Binomial when \(n \to \infty\) and \(p \to 0\) with \(np\) fixed—still how we justify Poisson approximations today.

The **Normal/Gaussian** curve emerged from **de Moivre** and **Laplace** as a Binomial limit, and from **Gauss** in astronomical measurement errors (*Theoria Motus*, 1809). **Categorical** outcomes appear in **Pearson**’s chi-square work on contingency tables and later in **McCullagh–Nelder** generalized linear models, where exponential-family distributions (Bernoulli, Binomial, Poisson, Normal) share a unified framework.

Machine learning inherited this vocabulary: logistic regression (Bernoulli/Categorical), Poisson regression for counts, Gaussian noise in least squares, and softmax as modern Categorical parameterization.

timeline.label: Bernoulli & Binomial (1713)
timeline.description: Formal theory of independent trials and success counts—foundation for binary and fixed-n modeling.

timeline.label: Poisson limit (1837)
timeline.description: Rare-event counts as a Binomial limit; still used to approximate low-rate processes.

timeline.label: Normal/Gaussian error law (1800s)
timeline.description: Bell curve for measurement error and sums; central limit theorem cemented its ubiquity.

timeline.label: GLMs & ML losses (1900s–2000s)
timeline.description: Exponential-family distributions linked to log-likelihood losses; softmax neural nets inherit Categorical modeling.
